revision.text:
'''埼玉沼'''（さきたまぬま）は、[[埼玉県]][[行田市]]の南東部（[[埼玉村|埼玉地区]]・[[太田村 (埼玉県北埼玉郡)|太田地区]]）にかつて所在していた[[沼]]である。
== 概要 == 埼玉沼は古くには尾崎沼と称されていた[[縦]]約10[[町 (単位)|町]]（約1090[[メートル|m]]）・[[横]]16町（約1745m）・[[面積]]約50町（約49.6[[ヘクタール|ha]]）の[[沼地]]であり、[[星川]]と[[忍川]]に挟まれた[[後背湿地]]として[[埼玉郡]]埼玉村・小針村・若小玉村・長野村にまたがり所在していた。この当時は[[忍藩]]諸村の[[遊水地|悪水溜井]]となっていた。後に'''小針沼'''（こばりぬま）と呼ばれていたが[[1696年]]（[[元禄]]9年）に小針村と埼玉村との間で沼の[[呼称問題|名称問題]]が発生し、[[江戸幕府|幕府]]の裁許によって'''埼玉沼'''へと改められたと伝えられている。その後[[1728年]]（[[享保]]13年）になると、幕府の命を受けた[[井沢弥惣兵衛]]と埼玉村・小針村・若小玉村・長野村の4村の[[住民]]らにより[[新田開発]]が行われた。工事は1728年（享保13年）2月に着工し、同年6月に竣工した。沼の中央に[[排水路]]として[[小針落]]が開削され、小針落は[[忍川#旧忍川|旧忍川]]を伏せ越し[[野通川]]へと至る流路形態となっている。[[新田]]の[[検地]]は[[筧播磨守正鋪]]により行われ、埼玉村・小針村・若小玉村・長野村の4村の[[持添新田]]となった。こうして新田開発後の沼と呼ばれる範囲は縦8町2間（約876m）・[[横]]3町3間（約333m）・[[周囲|沼周り]]26町42間（約2913m）となっていた。しかし水はけがあまり良くなく、[[1754年]]（[[宝暦]]4年）に新田の中央部に[[南北]]に貫く380間（約691m）の[[堤防]]が設置され、堤防の東側は[[耕地]]として利用され、西側は元の沼地のようになった。東側の耕地は縦5町26間（約593m）・横5町20間（約582m）・周囲23町46間（約2593m）の規模で、小針村・埼玉村の耕地となっていた。[[1948年]]（[[昭和]]23年）の時点では埼玉沼やその周辺に[[掘り上げ田]]が残され、確認することができる。<ref>[http://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 埼玉沼周辺 （1948年4月2日撮影）] - [[国土地理院]]ホームページ</ref>今日の埼玉沼は[[古代蓮の里]]や[[埼玉県行田浄水場]]、[[圃場整備事業]]のなされた通常の[[水田]]などに整備され、かつての沼地の面影はあまり残されていない。埼玉県行田浄水場と古代蓮の里との間に位置している[[埼玉県道364号上新郷埼玉線|県道上新郷埼玉線]]は380間（約691m）の堤防の名残である。現在では名称について再び'''小針沼'''とも称されている。
== 所在地 == * [[埼玉県]][[行田市]][[大字]][[埼玉村|埼玉]]・大字小針・大字若小玉・大字長野
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考資料 == * 『[[武蔵国郡村誌]] 第十五巻（254ページ）』 [[埼玉県立図書館]] 発行 昭和三十年三月二十八日 発行

'''埼玉沼'''（さきたまぬま）は、[[埼玉県]][[行田市]]の南東部（[[埼玉村|埼玉地区]]・[[太田村 (埼玉県北埼玉郡)|太田地区]]）にかつて所在していた[[沼]]である。
== 概要 == 埼玉沼は古くには尾崎沼と称されていた[[縦]]約10[[町 (単位)|町]]（約1090[[メートル|m]]）・[[横]]16町（約1745m）・[[面積]]約50町（約49.6[[ヘクタール|ha]]）の[[沼地]]であり、[[星川]]と[[忍川]]に挟まれた[[後背湿地]]として[[埼玉郡]]埼玉村・小針村・若小玉村・長野村にまたがり所在していた。この当時は[[忍藩]]諸村の[[遊水地|悪水溜井]]となっていた。後に'''小針沼'''（こばりぬま）と呼ばれていたが[[1696年]]（[[元禄]]9年）に小針村と埼玉村との間で沼の[[呼称問題|名称問題]]が発生し、[[江戸幕府|幕府]]の裁許によって'''埼玉沼'''へと改められたと伝えられている。その後[[1728年]]（[[享保]]13年）になると、幕府の命を受けた[[井沢弥惣兵衛]]と埼玉村・小針村・若小玉村・長野村の4村の[[住民]]らにより[[新田開発]]が行われた。工事は1728年（享保13年）2月に着工し、同年6月に竣工した。沼の中央に[[排水路]]として[[小針落]]が開削され、小針落は[[忍川#旧忍川|旧忍川]]を[[伏越|伏せ越し]][[野通川]]へと至る流路形態となっている。また、沼の北側には[[長野落]]が[[附廻堀]]として整備され、当初は[[見沼代用水]]へと至る流路となっていたが、後に旧忍川を伏せ越し、野通川へ流入する流路へと付け替えられている。[[新田]]の[[検地]]は[[筧播磨守正鋪]]により行われ、埼玉村・小針村・若小玉村・長野村の4村の[[持添新田]]となった。こうして新田開発後の沼と呼ばれる範囲は縦8町2間（約876m）・[[横]]3町3間（約333m）・[[周囲|沼周り]]26町42間（約2913m）となっていた。しかし水はけがあまり良くなく、たびたび水害が発生していたため、[[1754年]]（[[宝暦]]4年）に新田の中央部に[[南北]]に貫く380間（約691m）の中堤（なかつづみ）と称する[[堤防]]が設置され、堤防の東側の下沼（したぬま）は[[耕地]]として利用され、西側の上沼（うわぬま）は元の沼地のようになった。東側の耕地は縦5町26間（約593m）・横5町20間（約582m）・周囲23町46間（約2593m）の規模で、小針村・埼玉村の耕地となっていた。堤防の設置により、水害は減少した。[[1924年]]（[[大正]]13年）以後、沼地に戻されていた上沼において再び開田計画が起り、[[1934年]]（[[昭和]]9年）より[[1935年]]（昭和10年）まで工事が行われ、約27[[ヘクタール|ha]]の「昭和田（しょうわでん）」と称される[[水田]]となった。[[1948年]]（昭和23年）の時点では埼玉沼やその周辺に[[掘り上げ田]]が残され、確認することができる。<ref>[http://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 埼玉沼周辺 （1948年4月2日撮影）] - [[国土地理院]]ホームページ</ref>今日の埼玉沼は[[古代蓮の里]]や[[埼玉県行田浄水場]]、[[圃場整備事業]]のなされた通常の[[水田]]などに整備され、かつての沼地の面影はあまり残されていない。埼玉県行田浄水場と古代蓮の里との間に位置している[[埼玉県道364号上新郷埼玉線|県道上新郷埼玉線]]は380間（約691m）の堤防の名残である。現在では名称について再び'''小針沼'''とも称されている。
== 所在地 == * [[埼玉県]][[行田市]][[大字]][[埼玉村|埼玉]]・大字小針・大字若小玉・大字長野
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考資料 == * 『[[武蔵国郡村誌]] 第十五巻（254ページ）』 [[埼玉県立図書館]] 発行 昭和三十年三月二十八日 発行 * 『わたしたちのまち行田（131ページ ~ 133ページ）』 小学校社会科副読本編集委員会 編集 行田市教育委員会 発行 [[平成]]27年3月31日 発行
== 関連項目・周辺 == * [[埼玉県]] * [[行田市]] * [[古代蓮の里]] * [[埼玉県行田浄水場]] * 埼玉県水質管理センター * 小針クリーンセンター * [[さきたま古墳公園]] * [[さきたま古墳群]] * [[忍川#旧忍川|旧忍川]] * [[長野落]] * [[小針落]] * [[見沼代用水]]（[[星川]]） * [[埼玉県道364号上新郷埼玉線]] * [[井沢弥惣兵衛]] * [[小埼沼]]

revision.text:
'''埼玉沼'''（さきたまぬま）は、[[埼玉県]][[行田市]]の南東部（[[埼玉村|埼玉地区]]・[[太田村 (埼玉県北埼玉郡)|太田地区]]）にかつて所在していた[[沼]]である。
== 概要 == 埼玉沼は古くには尾崎沼と称されていた[[縦]]約10[[町 (単位)|町]]（約1090[[メートル|m]]）・[[横]]16町（約1745m）・[[面積]]約50町（約49.6[[ヘクタール|ha]]）の[[沼地]]であり、[[星川]]と[[忍川]]に挟まれた[[後背湿地]]として[[埼玉郡]]埼玉村・小針村・若小玉村・長野村にまたがり所在していた。この当時は[[忍藩]]諸村の[[遊水地|悪水溜井]]となっていた。後に'''小針沼'''（こばりぬま）と呼ばれていたが[[1696年]]（[[元禄]]9年）に小針村と埼玉村との間で沼の[[呼称問題|名称問題]]が発生し、[[江戸幕府|幕府]]の裁許によって'''埼玉沼'''へと改められたと伝えられている。その後[[1728年]]（[[享保]]13年）になると、幕府の命を受けた[[井沢弥惣兵衛]]と埼玉村・小針村・若小玉村・長野村の4村の[[住民]]らにより[[新田開発]]が行われた。工事は1728年（享保13年）2月に着工し、同年6月に竣工した。沼の中央に[[排水路]]として[[小針落]]が開削され、小針落は[[忍川#旧忍川|旧忍川]]を[[伏越|伏せ越し]][[野通川]]へと至る流路形態となっている。また、沼の北側には[[長野落]]が[[附廻堀]]として整備され、当初は[[見沼代用水]]へと至る流路となっていたが、後に旧忍川を伏せ越し、野通川へ流入する流路へと付け替えられている。[[新田]]の[[検地]]は[[筧播磨守正鋪]]により行われ、埼玉村・小針村・若小玉村・長野村の4村の[[持添新田]]となった。こうして新田開発後の沼と呼ばれる範囲は縦8町2間（約876m）・[[横]]3町3間（約333m）・[[周囲|沼周り]]26町42間（約2913m）となっていた。しかし水はけがあまり良くなく、たびたび水害が発生していたため、[[1754年]]（[[宝暦]]4年）に新田の中央部に[[南北]]に貫く380間（約691m）の中堤（なかつづみ）と称する[[堤防]]が設置され、堤防の東側の下沼（したぬま）は[[耕地]]として利用され、西側の上沼（うわぬま）は元の沼地のようになった。東側の耕地は縦5町26間（約593m）・横5町20間（約582m）・周囲23町46間（約2593m）の規模で、小針村・埼玉村の耕地となっていた。堤防の設置により、水害は減少した。[[1924年]]（[[大正]]13年）以後、沼地に戻されていた上沼において再び開田計画が起り、[[1934年]]（[[昭和]]9年）より[[1935年]]（昭和10年）まで工事が行われ、約27[[ヘクタール|ha]]の「昭和田（しょうわでん）」と称される[[水田]]となった。[[1948年]]（昭和23年）の時点では埼玉沼やその周辺に[[掘り上げ田]]が残され、確認することができる。<ref>[https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 埼玉沼周辺 （1948年4月2日撮影）] - [[国土地理院]]ホームページ</ref>今日の埼玉沼は[[古代蓮の里]]や[[埼玉県行田浄水場]]、[[圃場整備事業]]のなされた通常の[[水田]]などに整備され、かつての沼地の面影はあまり残されていない。埼玉県行田浄水場と古代蓮の里との間に位置している[[埼玉県道364号上新郷埼玉線|県道上新郷埼玉線]]は380間（約691m）の堤防の名残である。現在では名称について再び'''小針沼'''とも称されている。
== 所在地 == * [[埼玉県]][[行田市]][[大字]][[埼玉村|埼玉]]・大字小針・大字若小玉・大字長野
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考資料 == * 『[[武蔵国郡村誌]] 第十五巻（254ページ）』 [[埼玉県立図書館]] 発行 昭和三十年三月二十八日 発行 * 『わたしたちのまち行田（131ページ ~ 133ページ）』 小学校社会科副読本編集委員会 編集 行田市教育委員会 発行 [[平成]]27年3月31日 発行
== 関連項目・周辺 == * [[埼玉県]] * [[行田市]] * [[古代蓮の里]] * [[埼玉県行田浄水場]] * 埼玉県水質管理センター * 小針クリーンセンター * [[さきたま古墳公園]] * [[さきたま古墳群]] * [[忍川#旧忍川|旧忍川]] * [[長野落]] * [[小針落]] * [[見沼代用水]]（[[星川]]） * [[埼玉県道364号上新郷埼玉線]] * [[井沢弥惣兵衛]] * [[小埼沼]]

revision.text:
'''埼玉沼'''（さきたまぬま）は、[[埼玉県]][[行田市]]の南東部（[[埼玉村|埼玉地区]]・[[太田村 (埼玉県北埼玉郡)|太田地区]]）にかつて所在していた[[沼]]である。
== 概要 == 埼玉沼は古くには尾崎沼と称されていた[[縦]]約10[[町 (単位)|町]]（約1090[[メートル|m]]）・[[横]]16町（約1745m）・[[面積]]約50町（約49.6[[ヘクタール|ha]]）の[[沼地]]であり、[[星川]]と[[忍川]]に挟まれた[[後背湿地]]として[[埼玉郡]]埼玉村・小針村・若小玉村・長野村にまたがり所在していた。この当時は[[忍藩]]諸村の[[遊水地|悪水溜井]]となっていた。後に'''小針沼'''（こばりぬま）と呼ばれていたが[[1696年]]（[[元禄]]9年）に小針村と埼玉村との間で沼の[[呼称問題|名称問題]]が発生し、[[江戸幕府|幕府]]の裁許によって'''埼玉沼'''へと改められたと伝えられている。その後[[1728年]]（[[享保]]13年）になると、幕府の命を受けた[[井沢弥惣兵衛]]と埼玉村・小針村・若小玉村・長野村の4村の[[住民]]らにより[[新田開発]]が行われた。工事は1728年（享保13年）2月に着工し、同年6月に竣工した。沼の中央に[[排水路]]として[[小針落]]が開削され、小針落は[[忍川#旧忍川|旧忍川]]を[[伏越|伏せ越し]][[野通川]]へと至る流路形態となっている。また、沼の北側には[[長野落]]が[[附廻堀]]として整備され、当初は[[見沼代用水]]へと至る流路となっていたが、後に旧忍川を伏せ越し、野通川へ流入する流路へと付け替えられている。[[新田]]の[[検地]]は[[筧播磨守正鋪]]により行われ、埼玉村・小針村・若小玉村・長野村の4村の[[持添新田]]となった。こうして新田開発後の沼と呼ばれる範囲は縦8町2間（約876m）・[[横]]3町3間（約333m）・[[周囲|沼周り]]26町42間（約2913m）となっていた。しかし水はけがあまり良くなく、たびたび水害が発生していたため、[[1754年]]（[[宝暦]]4年）に新田の中央部に[[南北]]に貫く380間（約691m）の中堤（なかつづみ）と称する[[堤防]]が設置され、堤防の東側の下沼（したぬま）は[[耕地]]として利用され、西側の上沼（うわぬま）は元の沼地のようになった。東側の耕地は縦5町26間（約593m）・横5町20間（約582m）・周囲23町46間（約2593m）の規模で、小針村・埼玉村の耕地となっていた。堤防の設置により、水害は減少した。[[1924年]]（[[大正]]13年）以後、沼地に戻されていた上沼において再び開田計画が起り、[[1934年]]（[[昭和]]9年）より[[1935年]]（昭和10年）まで工事が行われ、約27[[ヘクタール|ha]]の「昭和田（しょうわでん）」と称される[[水田]]となった。[[1948年]]（昭和23年）の時点では埼玉沼やその周辺に[[掘り上げ田]]が残され、確認することができる。<ref>[https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 埼玉沼周辺 （1948年4月2日撮影）] - [[国土地理院]]ホームページ</ref>今日の埼玉沼は[[古代蓮の里]]や[[埼玉県行田浄水場]]、[[圃場整備事業]]のなされた通常の[[水田]]などに整備され、かつての沼地の面影はあまり残されていない。埼玉県行田浄水場と古代蓮の里との間に位置している[[埼玉県道364号上新郷埼玉線|県道上新郷埼玉線]]は380間（約691m）の堤防の名残である。現在では名称について再び'''小針沼'''とも称されている。
== 所在地 == * [[埼玉県]][[行田市]][[大字]][[埼玉村|埼玉]]・大字小針・大字若小玉・大字長野
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考資料 == * 『[[武蔵国郡村誌]] 第十五巻（254ページ）』 [[埼玉県立図書館]] 発行 昭和三十年三月二十八日 発行 * 『わたしたちのまち行田（131ページ ~ 133ページ）』 小学校社会科副読本編集委員会 編集 行田市教育委員会 発行 [[平成]]27年3月31日 発行
== 関連項目・周辺 == * [[埼玉県]] * [[行田市]] * [[古代蓮の里]] * [[埼玉県行田浄水場]] * 埼玉県水質管理センター * 小針クリーンセンター * [[さきたま古墳公園]] * [[さきたま古墳群]] * [[忍川#旧忍川|旧忍川]] * [[長野落]] * [[小針落]] * [[見沼代用水]]（[[星川]]） * [[埼玉県道364号上新郷埼玉線]] * [[井沢弥惣兵衛]] * [[小埼沼]]

revision.text:
'''埼玉沼'''（さきたまぬま）は、[[埼玉県]][[行田市]]の南東部（[[埼玉村|埼玉地区]]・[[太田村 (埼玉県北埼玉郡)|太田地区]]）にかつて所在していた[[沼]]である。
== 概要 == 埼玉沼は古くには尾崎沼と称されていた縦約10[[町 (単位)|町]]（約1090[[メートル|m]]）・横16町（約1745m）・[[面積]]約50町（約49.6[[ヘクタール|ha]]）の[[沼地]]であり、[[星川]]と[[忍川]]に挟まれた[[後背湿地]]として[[埼玉郡]]埼玉村・小針村・若小玉村・長野村にまたがり所在していた。この当時は[[忍藩]]諸村の[[遊水地|悪水溜井]]となっていた。後に'''小針沼'''（こばりぬま）と呼ばれていたが[[1696年]]（[[元禄]]9年）に小針村と埼玉村との間で沼の[[呼称問題|名称問題]]が発生し、[[江戸幕府|幕府]]の裁許によって'''埼玉沼'''へと改められたと伝えられている。その後[[1728年]]（[[享保]]13年）になると、幕府の命を受けた[[井沢弥惣兵衛]]と埼玉村・小針村・若小玉村・長野村の4村の[[住民]]らにより[[新田開発]]が行われた。工事は1728年（享保13年）2月に着工し、同年6月に竣工した。沼の中央に[[排水路]]として[[小針落]]が開削され、小針落は[[忍川#旧忍川|旧忍川]]を[[伏越|伏せ越し]][[野通川]]へと至る流路形態となっている。また、沼の北側には[[長野落]]が[[附廻堀]]として整備され、当初は[[見沼代用水]]へと至る流路となっていたが、後に旧忍川を伏せ越し、野通川へ流入する流路へと付け替えられている。[[新田]]の[[検地]]は[[筧播磨守正鋪]]により行われ、埼玉村・小針村・若小玉村・長野村の4村の[[持添新田]]となった。こうして新田開発後の沼と呼ばれる範囲は縦8町2間（約876m）・横3町3間（約333m）・[[周囲|沼周り]]26町42間（約2913m）となっていた。しかし水はけがあまり良くなく、たびたび水害が発生していたため、[[1754年]]（[[宝暦]]4年）に新田の中央部に南北に貫く380間（約691m）の中堤（なかつづみ）と称する[[堤防]]が設置され、堤防の東側の下沼（したぬま）は[[耕地]]として利用され、西側の上沼（うわぬま）は元の沼地のようになった。東側の耕地は縦5町26間（約593m）・横5町20間（約582m）・周囲23町46間（約2593m）の規模で、小針村・埼玉村の耕地となっていた。堤防の設置により、水害は減少した。[[1924年]]（[[大正]]13年）以後、沼地に戻されていた上沼において再び開田計画が起り、[[1934年]]（[[昭和]]9年）より[[1935年]]（昭和10年）まで工事が行われ、約27[[ヘクタール|ha]]の「昭和田（しょうわでん）」と称される[[水田]]となった。[[1948年]]（昭和23年）の時点では埼玉沼やその周辺に[[掘り上げ田]]が残され、確認することができる。<ref>[https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 埼玉沼周辺 （1948年4月2日撮影）] - [[国土地理院]]ホームページ</ref>今日の埼玉沼は[[古代蓮の里]]や[[埼玉県行田浄水場]]、[[圃場整備事業]]のなされた通常の[[水田]]などに整備され、かつての沼地の面影はあまり残されていない。埼玉県行田浄水場と古代蓮の里との間に位置している[[埼玉県道364号上新郷埼玉線|県道上新郷埼玉線]]は380間（約691m）の堤防の名残である。現在では名称について再び'''小針沼'''とも称されている。
== 所在地 == * [[埼玉県]][[行田市]][[大字]][[埼玉村|埼玉]]・大字小針・大字若小玉・大字長野
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考資料 == * 『[[武蔵国郡村誌]] 第十五巻（254ページ）』 [[埼玉県立図書館]] 発行 昭和三十年三月二十八日 発行 * 『わたしたちのまち行田（131ページ ~ 133ページ）』 小学校社会科副読本編集委員会 編集 行田市教育委員会 発行 [[平成]]27年3月31日 発行
== 関連項目・周辺 == * [[埼玉県]] * [[行田市]] * [[古代蓮の里]] * [[埼玉県行田浄水場]] * 埼玉県水質管理センター * 小針クリーンセンター * [[さきたま古墳公園]] * [[さきたま古墳群]] * [[忍川#旧忍川|旧忍川]] * [[長野落]] * [[小針落]] * [[見沼代用水]]（[[星川]]） * [[埼玉県道364号上新郷埼玉線]] * [[井沢弥惣兵衛]] * [[小埼沼]]

revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, Upper Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for Upper Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''
== Location == * [[Saitama Prefecture]] [[Gyōda]] [[Ōaza]] Sakitama, Ōaza Kobari, Ōaza Wakaodama, Ōaza Nagano
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * ''[[Musashi Province District and Village Records]], Volume 15 (p. 254)'' - Published by the [[Saitama Prefectural Library]], March 28, [[Shōwa]] 30 * ''Our Town Gyōda (pp. 131–133)'' - Edited by the Elementary School Social Studies Supplementary Reader Editorial Committee, published by the Gyōda City Board of Education, March 31, [[Heisei]] 27
== Related Topics and Surroundings == * [[Saitama Prefecture]] * [[Gyōda]] * [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] * [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]] * Saitama Prefecture Water Quality Management Center * Kobari Clean Center * [[Sakitama Burial Mounds Park]] * [[Sakitama Burial Mounds]] * [[Former Shinobu River]] * [[Nagano-Otoshi]] * [[Kobari-Otoshi]] * [[Minuma Canal]] ([[Hoshikawa River]]) * [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line) * [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] * [[Kozaki Marsh]]
== External Links == * [http://www.city.gyoda.lg.jp/41/03/10/bunkazai_itiran/hatimanyamakofunsekisitu.html "Hachimanyama Burial Mound Stone Chamber"] - Gyōda City Board of Education website * {{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.saitama.lg.jp/uploaded/attachment/407436.pdf "Kobari-Shitanuma Drainage Area"]}} - Saitama Prefecture website * {{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.saitama.lg.jp/uploaded/attachment/354131.pdf "Chara"]}} - Saitama Prefecture website * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/fukadasoft/renga/kobari/index2.html |title="Kobari-Otoshi" |date=20090817112417}} - Fukada Soft (Kimagure Travel Photo Gallery) * [http://habs.dc.affrc.go.jp/ Historical Agricultural Environment Viewing System] - [[National Agriculture and Food Research Organization]] website * [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] [https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/maplibSearch.do#1 Map and Aerial Photo Viewing Service] ** [https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=78535&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (November 3, 1947)] ** [https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] ...

revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).

'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).

However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, Upper Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for Upper Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, Upper Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for Upper Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:original pages]][[category:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[category:original pages]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, Upper Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for Upper Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''
== Location == * [[Saitama Prefecture]] [[Gyōda]] [[Ōaza]] Sakitama, Ōaza Kobari, Ōaza Wakaodama, Ōaza Nagano

revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, Upper Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for Upper Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''
== Location == * [[Saitama Prefecture]] [[Gyōda]] [[Ōaza]] Sakitama, Ōaza Kobari, Ōaza Wakaodama, Ōaza Nagano

revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, Upper Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for Upper Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''
== Location == * [[Saitama Prefecture]] [[Gyōda]] [[Ōaza]] Sakitama, Ōaza Kobari, Ōaza Wakaodama, Ōaza Nagano

revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, Upper Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for Upper Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, Upper Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for Upper Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, Upper Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for Upper Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, Upper Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for Upper Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, Upper Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for Upper Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''
== Location == * [[Saitama Prefecture]] [[Gyōda]] [[Ōaza]] Sakitama, Ōaza Kobari, Ōaza Wakaodama, Ōaza Nagano
== References == {{Reflist}}

'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, Upper Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for Upper Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''
== Location == * [[Saitama Prefecture]] [[Gyōda]] [[Ōaza]] Sakitama, Ōaza Kobari, Ōaza Wakaodama, Ōaza Nagano
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * ''[[Musashi Province District and Village Records]], Volume 15 (p. 254)'' - Published by the [[Saitama Prefectural Library]], March 28, [[Shōwa]] 30 * ''Our Town Gyōda (pp. 131–133)'' - Edited by the Elementary School Social Studies Supplementary Reader Editorial Committee, published by the Gyōda City Board of Education, March 31, [[Heisei]] 27
== Related Topics and Surroundings == * [[Saitama Prefecture]] * [[Gyōda]] * [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] * [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]] * Saitama Prefecture Water Quality Management Center * Kobari Clean Center * [[Sakitama Burial Mounds Park]] * [[Sakitama Burial Mounds]] * [[Former Shinobu River]] * [[Nagano-Otoshi]] * [[Kobari-Otoshi]] * [[Minuma Canal]] ([[Hoshikawa River]]) * [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line) * [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] * [[Kozaki Marsh]]

At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, Upper Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for Upper Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''
== Location == * [[Saitama Prefecture]] [[Gyōda]] [[Ōaza]] Sakitama, Ōaza Kobari, Ōaza Wakaodama, Ōaza Nagano
== References == {{Reflist}}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] to [[Category:qqqq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, Upper Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for Upper Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''
== Location == * [[Saitama Prefecture]] [[Gyōda]] [[Ōaza]] Sakitama, Ōaza Kobari, Ōaza Wakaodama, Ōaza Nagano
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * ''[[Musashi Province District and Village Records]], Volume 15 (p. 254)'' - Published by the [[Saitama Prefectural Library]], March 28, [[Shōwa]] 30 * ''Our Town Gyōda (pp. 131–133)'' - Edited by the Elementary School Social Studies Supplementary Reader Editorial Committee, published by the Gyōda City Board of Education, March 31, [[Heisei]] 27
== Related Topics and Surroundings == * [[Saitama Prefecture]] * [[Gyōda]] * [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] * [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]] * Saitama Prefecture Water Quality Management Center * Kobari Clean Center * [[Sakitama Burial Mounds Park]] * [[Sakitama Burial Mounds]] * [[Former Shinobu River]] * [[Nagano-Otoshi]] * [[Kobari-Otoshi]] * [[Minuma Canal]] ([[Hoshikawa River]]) * [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line) * [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] * [[Kozaki Marsh]]

revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, Upper Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for Upper Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''
== Location == * [[Saitama Prefecture]] [[Gyōda]] [[Ōaza]] Sakitama, Ōaza Kobari, Ōaza Wakaodama, Ōaza Nagano

After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, Upper Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for Upper Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, Upper Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for Upper Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, Upper Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for Upper Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland aqqroximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]).
