revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland aqqroximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were aqqroximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, Uqqer Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for Uqqer Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was aqqroximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://maqqs.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''
== Location == * [[Saitama Prefecture]] [[Gyōda]] [[Ōaza]] Sakitama, Ōaza Kobari, Ōaza Wakaodama, Ōaza Nagano
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * ''[[Musashi Province District and Village Records]], Volume 15 (p. 254)'' - Published by the [[Saitama Prefectural Library]], March 28, [[Shōwa]] 30 * ''Our Town Gyōda (qq. 131–133)'' - Edited by the Elementary School Social Studies Suqqlementary Reader Editorial Committee, published by the Gyōda City Board of Education, March 31, [[Heisei]] 27

revision.comment: Text replacement - "qqq" to "qq"
revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland aqqroximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were aqqroximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, Uqqer Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for Uqqer Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was aqqroximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://maqqs.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''
== Location == * [[Saitama Prefecture]] [[Gyōda]] [[Ōaza]] Sakitama, Ōaza Kobari, Ōaza Wakaodama, Ōaza Nagano

As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://maqqs.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland aqqroximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were aqqroximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, Uqqer Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for Uqqer Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was aqqroximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://maqqs.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''
== Location == * [[Saitama Prefecture]] [[Gyōda]] [[Ōaza]] Sakitama, Ōaza Kobari, Ōaza Wakaodama, Ōaza Nagano

Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were [[Category:pp error]]approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was [[Category:pp error]]approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland [[Category:pp error]]approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were [[Category:pp error]]approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was [[Category:pp error]]approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were [[Category:pp error]]approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was [[Category:pp error]]approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).

It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.

The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.

The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland [[Category:pp error]]approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were [[Category:pp error]]approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was [[Category:pp error]]approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

revision.comment: Text replacement - "ategory:completion tier" to "ategory:Completion tier"
revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

revision.comment: 7 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:埼玉沼 up to rev 100425230
revision.text:
'''埼玉沼'''（さきたまぬま）は、[[埼玉県]][[行田市]]の南東部（[[埼玉村|埼玉地区]]・[[太田村 (埼玉県北埼玉郡)|太田地区]]）にかつて所在していた[[沼]]である。
== 概要 == 埼玉沼は古くには尾崎沼と称されていた縦約10[[町 (単位)|町]]（約1090[[メートル|m]]）・横16町（約1745m）・[[面積]]約50町（約49.6[[ヘクタール|ha]]）の[[沼地]]であり、[[星川]]と[[忍川]]に挟まれた[[後背湿地]]として[[埼玉郡]]埼玉村・小針村・若小玉村・長野村にまたがり所在していた。この当時は[[忍藩]]諸村の[[遊水地|悪水溜井]]となっていた。後に'''小針沼'''（こばりぬま）と呼ばれていたが[[1696年]]（[[元禄]]9年）に小針村と埼玉村との間で沼の[[呼称問題|名称問題]]が発生し、[[江戸幕府|幕府]]の裁許によって'''埼玉沼'''へと改められたと伝えられている。その後[[1728年]]（[[享保]]13年）になると、幕府の命を受けた[[井沢弥惣兵衛]]と埼玉村・小針村・若小玉村・長野村の4村の[[住民]]らにより[[新田開発]]が行われた。工事は1728年（享保13年）2月に着工し、同年6月に竣工した。沼の中央に[[排水路]]として[[小針落]]が開削され、小針落は[[忍川#旧忍川|旧忍川]]を[[伏越|伏せ越し]][[野通川]]へと至る流路形態となっている。また、沼の北側には[[長野落]]が[[附廻堀]]として整備され、当初は[[見沼代用水]]へと至る流路となっていたが、後に旧忍川を伏せ越し、野通川へ流入する流路へと付け替えられている。[[新田]]の[[検地]]は[[筧播磨守正鋪]]により行われ、埼玉村・小針村・若小玉村・長野村の4村の[[持添新田]]となった。こうして新田開発後の沼と呼ばれる範囲は縦8町2間（約876m）・横3町3間（約333m）・[[周囲|沼周り]]26町42間（約2913m）となっていた。しかし水はけがあまり良くなく、たびたび水害が発生していたため、[[1754年]]（[[宝暦]]4年）に新田の中央部に南北に貫く380間（約691m）の中堤（なかつづみ）と称する[[堤防]]が設置され、堤防の東側の下沼（したぬま）は[[耕地]]として利用され、西側の上沼（うわぬま）は元の沼地のようになった。東側の耕地は縦5町26間（約593m）・横5町20間（約582m）・周囲23町46間（約2593m）の規模で、小針村・埼玉村の耕地となっていた。堤防の設置により、水害は減少した。[[1924年]]（[[大正]]13年）以後、沼地に戻されていた上沼において再び開田計画が起り、[[1934年]]（[[昭和]]9年）より[[1935年]]（昭和10年）まで工事が行われ、約27[[ヘクタール|ha]]の「昭和田（しょうわでん）」と称される[[水田]]となった。[[1948年]]（昭和23年）の時点では埼玉沼やその周辺に[[掘り上げ田]]が残され、確認することができる。<ref>[https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 埼玉沼周辺 （1948年4月2日撮影）] - [[国土地理院]]ホームページ</ref>今日の埼玉沼は[[古代蓮の里]]や[[埼玉県行田浄水場]]、[[圃場整備事業]]のなされた通常の[[水田]]などに整備され、かつての沼地の面影はあまり残されていない。埼玉県行田浄水場と古代蓮の里との間に位置している[[埼玉県道364号上新郷埼玉線|県道上新郷埼玉線]]は380間（約691m）の堤防の名残である。現在では名称について再び'''小針沼'''とも称されている。
== 所在地 == * [[埼玉県]][[行田市]][[大字]][[埼玉村|埼玉]]・大字小針・大字若小玉・大字長野
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考資料 == * 『[[武蔵国郡村誌]] 第十五巻（254ページ）』 [[埼玉県立図書館]] 発行 昭和三十年三月二十八日 発行 * 『わたしたちのまち行田（131ページ ~ 133ページ）』 小学校社会科副読本編集委員会 編集 行田市教育委員会 発行 [[平成]]27年3月31日 発行
== 関連項目・周辺 == * [[埼玉県]] * [[行田市]] * [[古代蓮の里]] * [[埼玉県行田浄水場]] * 埼玉県水質管理センター * 小針クリーンセンター * [[さきたま古墳公園]] * [[さきたま古墳群]] * [[忍川#旧忍川|旧忍川]] * [[長野落]] * [[小針落]] * [[見沼代用水]]（[[星川]]） * [[埼玉県道364号上新郷埼玉線]] * [[井沢弥惣兵衛]] * [[小埼沼]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.city.gyoda.lg.jp/41/03/10/bunkazai_itiran/hatimanyamakofunsekisitu.html 「八幡山古墳石室」] - 行田市教育委員会ホームページ * {{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.saitama.lg.jp/uploaded/attachment/407436.pdf 「小針下沼排水路地区」]}} - 埼玉県ホームページ * {{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.saitama.lg.jp/uploaded/attachment/354131.pdf 「シャジクモ」]}} - 埼玉県ホームページ * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/fukadasoft/renga/kobari/index2.html |title=「小針落」 |date=20090817112417}} - （有）フカダソフト（きまぐれ旅写真館） * [http://habs.dc.affrc.go.jp/ 『歴史的農業環境閲覧システム』] - [[独立行政法人]] [[農業環境技術研究所]]ホームページ * [[国土地理院]] [https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/maplibSearch.do#1 地図・空中写真閲覧サービス] ** [https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=78535&dispType=1 埼玉沼周辺 （1947年11月3日撮影）] ** [https://mapps.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 埼玉沼周辺 revision.comment: Bot: restore translated version
revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''
== Location == * [[Saitama Prefecture]] [[Gyōda]] [[Ōaza]] Sakitama, Ōaza Kobari, Ōaza Wakaodama, Ōaza Nagano

revision.comment: Bot: remove sole member from [[Category:Former marshes]]
revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''
== Location == * [[Saitama Prefecture]] [[Gyōda]] [[Ōaza]] Sakitama, Ōaza Kobari, Ōaza Wakaodama, Ōaza Nagano

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:行田市の歴史
revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''
== Location == * [[Saitama Prefecture]] [[Gyōda]] [[Ōaza]] Sakitama, Ōaza Kobari, Ōaza Wakaodama, Ōaza Nagano

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[:en:1696|1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[:en:1696|1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[:en:1728|1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[:en:1754|1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[:en:1924|1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[:en:1934|1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Marshes in Japan
revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[:en:1696|1696]] ([[Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[:en:1728|1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[:en:1754|1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[:en:1924|1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[:en:1934|1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[:en:1935|1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[:en:1948|1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[:en:Chō|Chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[:en:backswamp|backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[:en:1696|1696]] ([[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[:en:1728|1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[:en:1754|1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[:en:1924|1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[:en:1934|1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[:en:1935|1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[:en:1948|1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[:en:Chō|Chō]] (about 1,090 [[meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[:en:Hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[:en:backswamp|backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[:en:1696|1696]] ([[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[:en:1728|1728]] ([[Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[:en:1754|1754]] ([[Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[:en:1924|1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[:en:1934|1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[:en:1935|1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[:en:1948|1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[:en:Levee|Levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[:en:1754|1754]] ([[:en:Hōreki|Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[:en:1924|1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[:en:1934|1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[:en:1935|1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[:en:1948|1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

It was located as a [[:en:backswamp|backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. '''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[:en:marsh|marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[:en:Chō|Chō]] (about 1,090 [[:en:Meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[:en:Hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[:en:backswamp|backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[:en:1696|1696]] ([[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[:en:1728|1728]] ([[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[:en:Levee|Levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[:en:1754|1754]] ([[:en:Hōreki|Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[:en:1924|1924]] ([[Taishō]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[:en:1934|1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[:en:1935|1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[:en:1948|1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[:en:marsh|marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[:en:Chō|Chō]] (about 1,090 [[:en:Meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[:en:Hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[:en:backswamp|backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[:en:1696|1696]] ([[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[:en:1728|1728]] ([[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[:en:reclaimed land|reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[:en:Levee|Levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[:en:1754|1754]] ([[:en:Hōreki|Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[:en:1924|1924]] ([[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[:en:1934|1934]] ([[Shōwa]] 9) and [[:en:1935|1935]] ([[Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[:en:1948|1948]] ([[Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''
== Location == * [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]] [[:en:Gyōda|Gyōda]] [[Ōaza]] Sakitama, Ōaza Kobari, Ōaza Wakaodama, Ōaza Nagano

The result was approximately 27 ha of [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[:en:1948|1948]] ([[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q20044499]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[:en:marsh|marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[:en:Chō|Chō]] (about 1,090 [[:en:Meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[:en:Hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[:en:backswamp|backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[:en:retention basin|retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[:en:1696|1696]] ([[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[:en:1728|1728]] ([[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[:en:reclaimed land|reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[:en:Levee|Levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[:en:1754|1754]] ([[:en:Hōreki|Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[:en:1924|1924]] ([[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[:en:1934|1934]] ([[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 9) and [[:en:1935|1935]] ([[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[:en:1948|1948]] ([[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''
