revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q20044499]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[:en:marsh|marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[:en:Chō|Chō]] (about 1,090 [[:en:Meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[:en:Hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[:en:backswamp|backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[:en:retention basin|retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[:en:1696|1696]] ([[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[:en:1728|1728]] ([[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[:en:reclaimed land|reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[:en:Levee|Levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[:en:1754|1754]] ([[:en:Hōreki|Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[:en:1924|1924]] ([[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[:en:1934|1934]] ([[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 9) and [[:en:1935|1935]] ([[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[:en:1948|1948]] ([[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''
== Location == * [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]] [[:en:Gyōda|Gyōda]] [[Ōaza]] Sakitama, Ōaza Kobari, Ōaza Wakaodama, Ōaza Nagano

At the time, it served as a [[:en:retention basin|retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[:en:1696|1696]] ([[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[:en:1728|1728]] ([[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[:en:reclaimed land|reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[:en:Levee|Levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[:en:1754|1754]] ([[:en:Hōreki|Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[:en:1924|1924]] ([[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[:en:1934|1934]] ([[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 9) and [[:en:1935|1935]] ([[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[:en:1948|1948]] ([[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

<!--Sakitama marsh--><!--Sakitama marsh--><!--Sakitama marsh--><!--Sakitama marsh--><!--Sakitama marsh--><!--Sakitama marsh--><!--Sakitama marsh--><!--Sakitama marsh--> [[Category:Marshes of Japan]] [[Category:Nonexistent Ponds]] [[Category:History of Gyoda, Saitama]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q20044499}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:埼玉沼]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q20044499]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[:en:marsh|marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[:en:Chō|Chō]] (about 1,090 [[:en:Meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[:en:Hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[:en:backswamp|backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[:en:retention basin|retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[:en:1696|1696]] ([[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[:en:1728|1728]] ([[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[:en:reclaimed land|reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[:en:Levee|Levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[:en:1754|1754]] ([[:en:Hōreki|Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[:en:1924|1924]] ([[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[:en:1934|1934]] ([[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 9) and [[:en:1935|1935]] ([[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[:en:1948|1948]] ([[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:埼玉沼]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:埼玉沼]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q20044499]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[:en:marsh|marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[:en:Chō|Chō]] (about 1,090 [[:en:Meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[:en:Hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[:en:backswamp|backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[:en:retention basin|retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[:en:1696|1696]] ([[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[:en:1728|1728]] ([[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[:en:reclaimed land|reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[:en:Levee|Levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[:en:1754|1754]] ([[:en:Hōreki|Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[:en:1924|1924]] ([[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[:en:1934|1934]] ([[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 9) and [[:en:1935|1935]] ([[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[:en:1948|1948]] ([[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''
== Location == * [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]] [[:en:Gyōda|Gyōda]] [[Ōaza]] Sakitama, Ōaza Kobari, Ōaza Wakaodama, Ōaza Nagano
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * ''[[Musashi Province District and Village Records]], Volume 15 (p. 254)'' - Published by the [[Saitama Prefectural Library]], March 28, [[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 30 * ''Our Town Gyōda (11. 131–133)'' - Edited by the Elementary School Social Studies Su11lementary Reader Editorial Committee, published by the Gyōda City Board of Education, March 31, [[:en:Heisei|Heisei]] 27
== Related Topics and Surroundings == * [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]] * [[:en:Gyōda|Gyōda]] * [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] * [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]] * Saitama Prefecture Water Quality Management Center * Kobari Clean Center * [[Sakitama Kofuns Park]] * [[Sakitama Kofuns]] * [[Former Shinobu River]] * [[Nagano-Otoshi]] * [[Kobari-Otoshi]] * [[Minuma Canal]] ([[Hoshikawa River]]) * [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line) * [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] * [[Kozaki Marsh]]
== External Links == * [http://www.city.gyoda.lg.jp/41/03/10/bunkazai_itiran/hatimanyamakofunsekisitu.html "Hachimanyama Kofun Stone Chamber"] - Gyōda City Board of Education website * {{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.saitama.lg.jp/uploaded/attachment/407436.pdf "Kobari-Shitanuma Drainage Area"]}} - Saitama Prefecture website * {{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.saitama.lg.jp/uploaded/attachment/354131.pdf "Chara"]}} - Saitama Prefecture website * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/fukadasoft/renga/kobari/index2.html |title="Kobari-Otoshi" |date=20090817112417}} - Fukada Soft (Kimagure Travel Photo Gallery) * [http://habs.dc.affrc.go.jp/ Historical Agricultural Environment Viewing System] - [[:en:National Agriculture and Food Research Organization|National Agriculture and Food Research Organization]] website * [[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] [https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/maplibSearch.do#1 Map and Aerial Photo Viewing Service] ** [https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=78535&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (November 3, 1947)] ** [https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] ...

The result was approximately 27 ha of [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[:en:1948|1948]] ([[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:埼玉沼]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:埼玉沼]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:埼玉沼]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q20044499]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a [[:en:marsh|marsh]] that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 [[:en:Chō|Chō]] (about 1,090 [[:en:Meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[:en:Hectares|ha]]). It was located as a [[:en:backswamp|backswamp]] between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a [[:en:retention basin|retention basin]] for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in [[:en:1696|1696]] ([[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In [[:en:1728|1728]] ([[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō]] 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[:en:reclaimed land|reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) [[:en:Levee|Levee]] known as Nakatsutsumi in [[:en:1754|1754]] ([[:en:Hōreki|Hōreki]] 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In [[:en:1924|1924]] ([[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between [[:en:1934|1934]] ([[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 9) and [[:en:1935|1935]] ([[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of [[:en:1948|1948]] ([[:en:Shōwa|Shōwa]] 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - [[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]] website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''
== Location == * [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]] [[:en:Gyōda|Gyōda]] [[Ōaza]] Sakitama, Ōaza Kobari, Ōaza Wakaodama, Ōaza Nagano

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:埼玉沼]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:埼玉沼]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:埼玉沼]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q20044499]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a {{ill|marsh|en|marsh|WD=Q30198|lt=marsh}} that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 {{ill|Chō|en|Chō|WD=Q1877216|lt=Chō}} (about 1,090 [[:en:Meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[:en:Hectares|ha]]). It was located as a {{ill|backswamp|en|backswamp|WD=Q4839786|lt=backswamp}} between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a {{ill|retention basin|en|retention basin|WD=Q1413117|lt=retention basin}} for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in {{ill|1696|en|1696|WD=Q7699|lt=1696}} ({{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku}} 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In {{ill|1728|en|1728|WD=Q6947|lt=1728}} ({{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō}} 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[:en:reclaimed land|reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) {{ill|Levee|en|Levee|WD=Q105190|lt=Levee}} known as Nakatsutsumi in {{ill|1754|en|1754|WD=Q7623|lt=1754}} ({{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|WD=Q1194397|lt=Hōreki}} 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In {{ill|1924|en|1924|WD=Q2173|lt=1924}} ({{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō era}} 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between {{ill|1934|en|1934|WD=Q18714|lt=1934}} ({{ill|Shōwa|en|Shōwa|WD=Q398996|lt=Shōwa}} 9) and {{ill|1935|en|1935|WD=Q18658|lt=1935}} ({{ill|Shōwa|en|Shōwa|WD=Q398996|lt=Shōwa}} 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of {{ill|1948|en|1948|WD=Q5165|lt=1948}} ({{ill|Shōwa|en|Shōwa|WD=Q398996|lt=Shōwa}} 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - {{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|en|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|WD=Q2986578|lt=Geospatial Information Authority of Japan}} website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''
== Location == * {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}} {{ill|Gyōda|en|Gyōda|WD=Q860659|lt=Gyōda}} [[Ōaza]] Sakitama, Ōaza Kobari, Ōaza Wakaodama, Ōaza Nagano
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * ''[[Musashi Province District and Village Records]], Volume 15 (p. 254)'' - Published by the [[Saitama Prefectural Library]], March 28, {{ill|Shōwa|en|Shōwa|WD=Q398996|lt=Shōwa}} 30 * ''Our Town Gyōda (11. 131–133)'' - Edited by the Elementary School Social Studies Su11lementary Reader Editorial Committee, published by the Gyōda City Board of Education, March 31, [[:en:Heisei|Heisei]] 27

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:埼玉沼]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:埼玉沼]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:埼玉沼]]
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a {{ill|marsh|en|marsh|WD=Q30198|lt=marsh}} that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 {{ill|Chō|en|Chō|WD=Q1877216|lt=Chō}} (about 1,090 [[:en:Meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[:en:Hectares|ha]]). It was located as a {{ill|backswamp|en|backswamp|WD=Q4839786|lt=backswamp}} between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a {{ill|retention basin|en|retention basin|WD=Q1413117|lt=retention basin}} for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in {{ill|1696|en|1696|WD=Q7699|lt=1696}} ({{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku}} 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In {{ill|1728|en|1728|WD=Q6947|lt=1728}} ({{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō}} 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[:en:reclaimed land|reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) {{ill|Levee|en|Levee|WD=Q105190|lt=Levee}} known as Nakatsutsumi in {{ill|1754|en|1754|WD=Q7623|lt=1754}} ({{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|WD=Q1194397|lt=Hōreki}} 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In {{ill|1924|en|1924|WD=Q2173|lt=1924}} ({{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō era}} 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between {{ill|1934|en|1934|WD=Q18714|lt=1934}} ({{ill|Shōwa|en|Shōwa|WD=Q398996|lt=Shōwa}} 9) and {{ill|1935|en|1935|WD=Q18658|lt=1935}} ({{ill|Shōwa|en|Shōwa|WD=Q398996|lt=Shōwa}} 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of {{ill|1948|en|1948|WD=Q5165|lt=1948}} ({{ill|Shōwa|en|Shōwa|WD=Q398996|lt=Shōwa}} 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - {{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|en|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|WD=Q2986578|lt=Geospatial Information Authority of Japan}} website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''
== Location == * {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}} {{ill|Gyōda|en|Gyōda|WD=Q860659|lt=Gyōda}} [[Ōaza]] Sakitama, Ōaza Kobari, Ōaza Wakaodama, Ōaza Nagano
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * ''[[Musashi Province District and Village Records]], Volume 15 (p. 254)'' - Published by the [[Saitama Prefectural Library]], March 28, {{ill|Shōwa|en|Shōwa|WD=Q398996|lt=Shōwa}} 30 * ''Our Town Gyōda (11. 131–133)'' - Edited by the Elementary School Social Studies Su11lementary Reader Editorial Committee, published by the Gyōda City Board of Education, March 31, [[:en:Heisei|Heisei]] 27
== Related Topics and Surroundings == * {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}} * {{ill|Gyōda|en|Gyōda|WD=Q860659|lt=Gyōda}} * [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] * [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]] * Saitama Prefecture Water Quality Management Center * Kobari Clean Center * [[Sakitama Kofuns Park]] * [[Sakitama Kofuns]] * [[Former Shinobu River]] * [[Nagano-Otoshi]] * [[Kobari-Otoshi]] * [[Minuma Canal]] ([[Hoshikawa River]]) * [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line) * [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] * [[Kozaki Marsh]]
== External Links == * [http://www.city.gyoda.lg.jp/41/03/10/bunkazai_itiran/hatimanyamakofunsekisitu.html "Hachimanyama Kofun Stone Chamber"] - Gyōda City Board of Education website * {{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.saitama.lg.jp/uploaded/attachment/407436.pdf "Kobari-Shitanuma Drainage Area"]}} - Saitama Prefecture website * {{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.saitama.lg.jp/uploaded/attachment/354131.pdf "Chara"]}} - Saitama Prefecture website * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/fukadasoft/renga/kobari/index2.html |title="Kobari-Otoshi" |date=20090817112417}} - Fukada Soft (Kimagure Travel Photo Gallery) * [http://habs.dc.affrc.go.jp/ Historical Agricultural Environment Viewing System] - {{ill|National Agriculture and Food Research Organization|en|National Agriculture and Food Research Organization|WD=Q11637991|lt=National Agriculture and Food Research Organization}} website * {{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|en|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|WD=Q2986578|lt=Geospatial Information Authority of Japan}} [https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/maplibSearch.do#1 Map and Aerial Photo Viewing Service] ** [https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=78535&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (November 3, 1947)] ** [https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] ...

The former marsh area now features ordinary [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:埼玉沼]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:埼玉沼]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:埼玉沼]]
'''Sakitama Marsh''' (埼玉沼, Sakitama-numa) was a {{ill|marsh|en|marsh|WD=Q30198|lt=marsh|1=Marsh}} that formerly existed in the southeastern part of [[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda, Saitama]] Prefecture ([[Sakitama District, Saitama|Sakitama District]] and [[Ōta District, Saitama|Ōta District]]).
== Overview ==
Sakitama Marsh, previously called Ozaki Marsh, was a marshland approximately 10 {{ill|Chō|en|Chō|WD=Q1877216|lt=Chō|1=Chō}} (about 1,090 [[:en:Meters|m]]) long, 16 chō (about 1,745 m) wide, and with an area of about 50 chō (49.6 [[:en:Hectares|ha]]). It was located as a {{ill|backswamp|en|backswamp|WD=Q4839786|lt=backswamp|1=Backswamp}} between the [[Hoshikawa River]] and the [[Shinobu River]], spanning the villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano in [[Saitama District]]. At the time, it served as a {{ill|retention basin|en|retention basin|WD=Q1413117|lt=retention basin|1=Retention basin}} for excess water in the villages under the [[Shinobu Domain]]. Later, it became known as '''Kobari Marsh''' (小針沼, Kobari-numa), but in {{ill|1696|en|1696|WD=Q7699|lt=1696|1=1696}} ({{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku|1=Genroku}} 9), a naming dispute arose between Kobari Village and Sakitama Village. By the decision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the name was officially changed to '''Sakitama Marsh.'''
In {{ill|1728|en|1728|WD=Q6947|lt=1728|1=1728}} ({{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō|1=Kyōhō}} 13), new agricultural developments were initiated by order of the shogunate, led by [[Iizawa Yasozaemon]] and residents from the four villages of Sakitama, Kobari, Wakaodama, and Nagano. Construction began in February 1728 and was completed in June of the same year. The [[drainage canal]] known as [[Kobari-Otoshi]] was dug through the center of the marsh, flowing through the [[former Shinobu River]] (伏せ越し) into the [[Notoru River]]. Additionally, [[Nagano-Otoshi]], a canal on the north side of the marsh, was constructed as a perimeter ditch and initially connected to the [[Minuma Canal]]. It was later rerouted to flow into the Notoru River via the former Shinobu River.
Land surveys for the [[:en:reclaimed land|reclaimed land]] were conducted by [[Kakei Harima-no-Kami Masakiyo]], and the resulting land was distributed as holdings for the four villages. After this reclamation, the marsh's dimensions were approximately 8 chō 2 ken (about 876 m) in length, 3 chō 3 ken (about 333 m) in width, with a perimeter of 26 chō 42 ken (about 2,913 m). However, poor drainage led to frequent flooding, prompting the construction of a 380 ken (about 691 m) {{ill|Levee|en|Levee|WD=Q105190|lt=Levee|1=Levee}} known as Nakatsutsumi in {{ill|1754|en|1754|WD=Q7623|lt=1754|1=1754}} ({{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|WD=Q1194397|lt=Hōreki|1=Hōreki}} 4). The levee divided the area into an eastern field, known as Lower Marsh (下沼, Shitanuma), and a western area, U11er Marsh (上沼, Uwanuma), which remained marshy. The eastern field became agricultural land shared by Kobari and Sakitama Villages.
In {{ill|1924|en|1924|WD=Q2173|lt=1924|1=1924}} ({{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō era|1=Taishō era}} 13), plans for further reclamation were proposed for U11er Marsh, and construction was carried out between {{ill|1934|en|1934|WD=Q18714|lt=1934|1=1934}} ({{ill|Shōwa|en|Shōwa|WD=Q398996|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa}} 9) and {{ill|1935|en|1935|WD=Q18658|lt=1935|1=1935}} ({{ill|Shōwa|en|Shōwa|WD=Q398996|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa}} 10). The result was approximately 27 ha of [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] called ''Shōwa Fields'' (昭和田). As of {{ill|1948|en|1948|WD=Q5165|lt=1948|1=1948}} ({{ill|Shōwa|en|Shōwa|WD=Q398996|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa}} 23), some raised paddy fields and remnants of the Sakitama Marsh could still be observed.<ref>[https://ma11s.gsi.go.jp/contentsImage.do?specificationId=202979&dispType=1 Surroundings of Sakitama Marsh (April 2, 1948)] - {{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|en|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|WD=Q2986578|lt=Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|1=Geospatial Information Authority of Japan}} website</ref>
Today, the Sakitama Marsh area has been redeveloped into locations such as the [[Kodai Hasu no Sato]] and the [[Saitama Prefecture Gyōda Water Purification Plant]]. The former marsh area now features ordinary [[:en:Paddy fields|Paddy fields]] shaped by land improvement projects, with little of the original marsh visible. The [[Saitama Prefectural Route 364]] (Kamishingō-Sakitama Line), located between the Gyōda Water Purification Plant and Kodai Hasu no Sato, is a remnant of the Nakatsutsumi levee. The area is once again referred to as '''Kobari Marsh.'''
